title: Debt and efficiency ratio analysis
summary: Profit alone never tells you whether a business is safe or well run. This HL-only lesson works through the whole of syllabus 3.6: the gearing ratio, which reveals how a business is financed and how exposed it is to the risk of debt; and the three efficiency ratios — stock (inventory) turnover, debtor (receivables) days and creditor (payables) days — which show how well a firm manages its working-capital cycle. You will calculate each ratio from real figures, interpret what the number means in context, choose strategies to improve it, and weigh the limitations of ratio analysis. Because 3.6 is a QUANTITATIVE topic, every worked example is set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks — a mark (M) for the correct method, an accuracy mark (A) for the correct value with its unit, further marks for a valid interpretation, and follow-through on a correct method built on a wrong earlier figure — so you can see exactly where each mark is won and lost.
learningObjectives: Calculate and interpret the gearing ratio to judge how a business is financed and its exposure to financial risk.
Calculate and interpret the efficiency ratios — stock (inventory) turnover, debtor (receivables) days and creditor (payables) days — using the correct formula and units.
Recommend and justify strategies to improve a firm's gearing and efficiency ratios in context.
Evaluate the limitations of ratio analysis and the common errors examiners penalise.

simpleExplanation.title: The dashboard behind the profit figure
simpleExplanation.summary: Debt and efficiency ratios are the warning lights on a business's dashboard. Gearing tells you how much of the engine is running on borrowed fuel — useful for speed, dangerous if the price of fuel (interest) jumps. The efficiency ratios are the speedometers of the working-capital cycle: how fast stock sells, how fast customers pay you, and how slowly you can pay your suppliers. A business can look profitable on the surface and still stall if these gauges are in the red.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you run a market stall selling trainers. Gearing is the share of your stall that you set up with a loan from the bank rather than your own savings — borrow a lot and you can stock more shelves, but every month the interest is due whether or not you sell a single pair. Stock turnover is how many times you clear and refill the shelves in a year; debtor days is how long a customer who bought 'on tick' takes to actually hand over the cash; and creditor days is how long your supplier lets you wait before paying for the trainers. You stay liquid by selling fast, collecting from customers quickly, and paying your supplier a little more slowly.
steps: Pick the correct formula for the ratio the question asks for — gearing uses capital employed; stock turnover and creditor days use cost of goods sold; debtor days uses sales revenue.
Pull the exact figures from the case study's financial statements and substitute them in, showing your working.
Calculate accurately and attach the correct unit — a percentage for gearing, 'times' or 'days' for the efficiency ratios.
Interpret the result in context — compare it to previous years, competitors or industry norms — and, if asked, suggest a realistic way to improve it.

flashcards.back: The proportion of a firm's capital employed that is financed by long-term (non-current) debt. It shows how a business is financed and how exposed it is to financial risk from interest and repayment obligations.
Gearing (%) = (Long-term liabilities ÷ Capital employed) × 100. Capital employed = long-term liabilities + share capital + retained earnings (i.e. non-current liabilities + total equity).

Above roughly 50% is usually treated as highly geared — more than half the capital is debt. It raises the risk that rising interest rates or a downturn make fixed interest and repayments unaffordable, but it can also fuel faster growth (financial leverage).
An efficiency ratio measuring how quickly a firm sells and replaces its stock — either the number of times per year, or the average number of days stock is held.
In times per year: Cost of goods sold ÷ Average stock. In days: (Average stock ÷ Cost of goods sold) × 365. Average stock = (opening stock + closing stock) ÷ 2.
The average number of days a business takes to collect cash from credit customers after a sale. A lower figure is better for cash flow.
Debtor days = (Debtors ÷ Sales revenue) × 365. Note the denominator is SALES REVENUE, not cost of goods sold.
The average number of days a business takes to pay its suppliers. A higher figure eases cash flow, but taken too far it can damage supplier relationships.
Creditor days = (Creditors ÷ Cost of goods sold) × 365. The denominator is COST OF GOODS SOLD, not sales revenue.
Debtor days uses SALES REVENUE. Stock turnover and creditor days both use COST OF GOODS SOLD. Swapping the denominators is one of the most common and most costly errors.
Ideally debtor days < creditor days: the firm collects cash from customers before it has to pay suppliers, so supplier credit helps finance the working-capital cycle and protects liquidity.

flashcards.front: Trap: a 'good' ratio is not a fixed number
flashcards.back: There is no universal correct value. A jeweller's slow stock turnover is normal; a supermarket's would be alarming. Always judge a ratio against the firm's own trend, its competitors and its industry.

faq.a: No. High gearing raises financial risk — fixed interest and repayments must be met whatever profits do, so a highly geared firm is vulnerable to rising interest rates and downturns. But gearing up can be a deliberate strategy (financial leverage): if the return earned on borrowed funds exceeds the interest paid, debt magnifies returns to shareholders and can fund faster growth than equity alone. Whether it is wise depends on the stability of profits, the interest rate and the industry — capital-intensive firms routinely run higher gearing than service firms.

faq.q: How can a business reduce its gearing ratio?
faq.a: Two routes: cut the long-term debt or grow the equity. A firm can repay or refinance long-term loans, issue new shares to raise equity, or retain more profit rather than paying it out as dividends, which increases retained earnings and so capital employed. Each lowers the debt share of capital employed. The trade-off is that issuing shares dilutes existing owners' control and retaining profit reduces dividends.

faq.q: What can a business do to improve its debtor days?
faq.a: To collect cash faster (lower debtor days) a firm can tighten credit control and vet customers before extending credit, offer small discounts for early payment, invoice promptly and chase overdue accounts, shorten the credit period offered, or use debt factoring to get cash for invoices immediately. Each has a cost — discounts and factoring reduce the amount received, and stricter terms may push price-sensitive customers to competitors — so the fix must suit the business.

faq.q: How do I know whether a ratio result is 'good' or 'bad'?
faq.a: Context decides. A ratio is only meaningful when compared against something: the firm's own results in previous years (is it improving or deteriorating?), direct competitors, and the industry norm. A stock turnover of 60 days is fine for a furniture retailer but poor for a grocer. Never label a single number 'good' or 'bad' in isolation — the analysis, not the arithmetic, earns the marks.

faq.q: Why isn't ratio analysis enough on its own?
faq.a: Ratios are built from historical accounts, so they describe the past, not the future, and can be distorted by inflation, one-off events, different accounting policies or window dressing. They ignore qualitative factors — staff morale, brand strength, market conditions, the quality of management — that often decide whether a firm succeeds. Ratios are a starting point for investigation, not a verdict; a strong answer uses them alongside qualitative judgement and states their limitations.

sections.content: A business can be profitable and still collapse — because profit is not the same as safety, and it is not the same as cash. Two questions decide whether a profitable firm actually survives: how is it financed, and how well does it manage the day-to-day flow of stock and cash? This HL-only lesson answers both. Gearing analysis shows how much of the business is built on debt and how exposed that leaves it to interest and repayment risk. Efficiency (activity) analysis — stock turnover, debtor days and creditor days — shows how quickly the firm turns stock into sales and sales into cash. Because 3.6 is a quantitative topic, the worked examples are set out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks: an M mark for the right method, an A mark for the accurate value with its unit, and further marks for a valid interpretation — so you can see where every mark is earned.
The gearing ratio: how a business is financed

The gearing ratio measures the proportion of a firm's capital employed that comes from long-term debt rather than from the owners' equity. It answers a single, important question: is this business financed mainly by money it owes, or by money it owns? A highly geared firm carries large fixed interest payments and repayment obligations that fall due whether or not it is trading well, which makes it more vulnerable to rising interest rates and economic downturns. A low-geared firm is safer but may be leaving growth on the table by not using cheap debt. Gearing is therefore a measure of long-term financial risk, and how a business is financed.

items: **Capital employed** = long-term (non-current) liabilities + total equity (share capital + retained earnings). It is the total long-term funding the business is using.
**Low gearing (below ~25%):** financed mainly by equity — safe and resilient, but possibly under-using debt to grow.
**High gearing (above ~50%):** more than half the capital is debt — riskier, with heavy fixed interest, but potentially faster-growing through financial leverage.

**No universal 'right' level:** capital-intensive industries (utilities, airlines) run high gearing normally; service firms usually run low. Always judge against the industry and the firm's own trend.

sections.question:
A company reports the following long-term funding at 31 December 2025:

- Long-term liabilities (loan capital): $400,000
- Share capital: $350,000
- Retained earnings: $250,000

Calculate the gearing ratio and comment on the result. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

**Step 1 — Capital employed.**
Capital employed = long-term liabilities + share capital + retained earnings
= $400,000 + $350,000 + $250,000 = $1,000,000.

**Step 2 — Gearing ratio.**
Gearing = (long-term liabilities ÷ capital employed) × 100
= ($400,000 ÷ $1,000,000) × 100 = **40%**.

**Comment.** At 40% the company is moderately geared: 40 cents of every dollar of long-term capital is borrowed. This is below the 50% level usually treated as high, so financial risk is manageable, yet the firm is still using debt to help fund its operations. Whether 40% is comfortable depends on how stable its profits are and on interest rates — if earnings are volatile, even this level of fixed interest could become a strain.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — this is a quantitative response marked by M (method) and A (accuracy) conventions:

- **M1 (method):** correct gearing method — long-term liabilities over capital employed, with capital employed correctly formed as $1,000,000.
- **A1 (accuracy):** correct value **40%**, with the % unit.
- **A1 (interpretation):** a valid reading of the number — e.g. moderately geared, below the ~50% high-gearing threshold, risk manageable.
- **A1 (context/development):** the interpretation is developed against context — reliance on debt, sensitivity to profit stability or interest rates. The engine credits the correct method even if a wrong capital-employed figure feeds into it (follow-through), but the accuracy mark needs the right value with its unit.

sections.content: When a firm is uncomfortably highly geared, it can move the ratio in two directions: shrink the debt (the numerator) or grow the equity (part of the denominator). Which route suits the business depends on its access to capital markets, its dividend policy and how much control the owners are willing to share.

items: **Repay or refinance long-term loans:** using retained profit or cash to pay down debt directly cuts the numerator — but consumes cash the firm may need elsewhere.
**Issue new shares (raise equity):** increases capital employed and lowers the debt share — but dilutes existing owners' control and, for a plc, invites market scrutiny.
**Retain more profit:** keeping earnings rather than paying dividends raises retained earnings and so capital employed — but disappoints shareholders wanting income.
**Judge in context:** a firm with volatile profits has a stronger case to de-gear; a stable, capital-intensive firm may reasonably keep high gearing to fund growth.

sections.content: Efficiency ratios: managing the working-capital cycle

Efficiency ratios (also called activity ratios) measure how well a firm uses its assets and manages its short-term liabilities — the heart of working-capital management. The three in the syllabus map onto the working-capital cycle: the time it takes to turn stock into cash. Stock turnover shows how fast goods sell; debtor days shows how fast customers pay; creditor days shows how slowly the firm can pay its suppliers. Managing this cycle well is what keeps a profitable firm liquid.

ext{Stock turnover (times)} =  rac{	ext{Cost of goods sold}}{	ext{Average stock}} 
ewline 
ewline 	ext{Stock turnover (days)} =  rac{	ext{Average stock}}{	ext{Cost of goods sold}} 	imes 365 
ewline 
ewline 	ext{Debtor days} =  rac{	ext{Debtors}}{	ext{Sales revenue}} 	imes 365 
ewline 
ewline 	ext{Creditor days} =  rac{	ext{Creditors}}{	ext{Cost of goods sold}} 	imes 365

items: **Stock (inventory) turnover:** more times per year (or fewer days) usually signals efficient selling and less capital tied up in stock — but the ideal depends on the industry. A grocer needs rapid turnover; a jeweller or car dealer normally has slow turnover of high-value items.
**Debtor (receivables) days:** lower is better — cash arrives sooner, protecting liquidity. Rising debtor days can signal weak credit control or customers in difficulty.

**Creditor (payables) days:** higher generally helps cash flow, as supplier credit finances the cycle — but paying too slowly risks losing discounts, damaging supplier goodwill or losing suppliers altogether.
**The target relationship:** aim for debtor days < creditor days, so cash comes in from customers before it must go out to suppliers — a self-financing, liquidity-friendly cycle.

sections.question:
For the year ended 31 December 2025, 'Meridian Traders' reports:

- Sales revenue: $1,200,000
- Cost of goods sold: $720,000
- Opening stock: $110,000
- Closing stock: $130,000
- Debtors (trade receivables): $150,000
- Creditors (trade payables): $90,000

Calculate the stock turnover (in days), debtor days and creditor days, and comment on the firm's working-capital management. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

**1. Stock turnover (days).**
Average stock = ($110,000 + $130,000) ÷ 2 = $120,000.
Stock turnover = (average stock ÷ COGS) × 365 = ($120,000 ÷ $720,000) × 365 = **60.8 ≈ 61 days**.
Meridian holds stock for about 61 days before selling it.

**2. Debtor days.**
Debtor days = (debtors ÷ sales revenue) × 365 = ($150,000 ÷ $1,200,000) × 365 = **45.6 ≈ 46 days**.
It takes about 46 days to collect cash from credit customers.

**3. Creditor days.**
Creditor days = (creditors ÷ COGS) × 365 = ($90,000 ÷ $720,000) × 365 = **45.6 ≈ 46 days**.
Meridian takes about 46 days to pay its suppliers.

**Comment.** Debtor days (46) and creditor days (46) are almost identical, so Meridian is collecting from customers at roughly the same speed as it pays suppliers — its supplier credit no longer finances the cycle, leaving little liquidity cushion. Ideally it would push creditor days above debtor days. The 61-day stock turnover also looks slow for a trading business and ties up $120,000 of capital in stock, raising storage and obsolescence risk. Priorities: tighten credit control to cut debtor days and reduce stock holding.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:**

- **M1:** correct stock-turnover method, including average stock formed as $120,000. **A1:** value **≈ 61 days** with unit.
- **M1:** correct debtor-days method (debtors ÷ **sales revenue** × 365). **A1:** value **≈ 46 days** with unit.
- **M1:** correct creditor-days method (creditors ÷ **COGS** × 365) — combined with **A1** for a valid interpretation of the three figures together (e.g. debtor ≈ creditor days gives no cash cushion; slow stock turnover ties up capital).
- The engine applies **follow-through**: a correct method using a wrongly calculated average stock still earns the method mark. Using the wrong denominator — sales revenue for creditor days, or COGS for debtor days — forfeits the corresponding method mark.

sections.content: Watch the denominators: COGS for stock turnover and creditor days, sales revenue for debtor days. And when the question gives opening AND closing stock, you must first find average stock ((opening + closing) ÷ 2) before the stock-turnover calculation — using a single stock figure when both are provided loses the method mark. Always end with a unit: %, times, or days.
Strategies to improve the efficiency ratios

items: **Speed up stock turnover:** adopt just-in-time or lean stock control, clear slow-moving lines with discounts, improve demand forecasting, and drop obsolete products — but avoid stockouts that lose sales.

**Reduce debtor days:** tighten credit control and vet customers, offer early-payment discounts, invoice promptly and chase overdue accounts, shorten credit terms, or use debt factoring — accepting that discounts and factoring reduce the cash received.

**Lengthen creditor days (carefully):** negotiate longer payment terms with suppliers to keep cash longer — but not so far that you lose early-payment discounts or damage supplier relationships and reliability.
**Aim for the whole cycle:** the goal is a shorter cash cycle overall — fast stock turnover, quick collection from debtors, and reasonable (not reckless) delay in paying creditors.

sections.content: Ratios are powerful, but they are a torch, not a verdict. They are only as good as the accounts they come from, they describe the past, and they say nothing about the qualitative factors that often decide a firm's future. A strong HL answer uses ratios to open an investigation and then names their limits rather than treating a single number as proof.

items: **Backward-looking:** ratios are built from historical accounts and describe what has already happened, not what will happen next.
**Comparison problems:** different firms use different accounting policies (e.g. depreciation, stock valuation), and inflation distorts figures over time, so like-for-like comparison is hard.
**No qualitative factors:** ratios ignore staff morale, brand strength, management quality, innovation and market conditions — often the real drivers of success or failure.
**Can be distorted:** one-off events and 'window dressing' of accounts near the year-end can flatter or distort ratios.
**Context-dependent:** the same value is healthy in one industry and alarming in another, so a ratio means nothing without a benchmark — the firm's trend, competitors or industry norms.

**Confusing which denominator to use** — debtor days uses SALES REVENUE; stock turnover and creditor days use COST OF GOODS SOLD. Swapping them is the single most common efficiency-ratio error and forfeits the method mark.

**Thinking high gearing simply means 'more debt is always bad'** — high gearing means MORE RISK, but it can also mean faster growth through leverage. State the risk AND the trade-off, not a blanket 'bad'.
**Dropping the unit** — a bare number with no %, 'times' or 'days' loses the accuracy mark. Every ratio needs its unit.
**Forgetting to average the stock** — when opening and closing stock are both given, average them before calculating stock turnover; using one figure loses the method mark.
**Calling a ratio 'good' or 'bad' with no benchmark** — a number in isolation earns little; the marks come from comparing it to the firm's trend, competitors or industry.

**Mixing up gearing capital** — gearing uses LONG-TERM (non-current) liabilities over CAPITAL EMPLOYED, not current liabilities or total assets. Capital employed = long-term liabilities + total equity.
**Calculating without interpreting** — a correct number with no comment cannot reach the interpretation marks; always say what the figure means for THIS business.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Because 3.6 is a quantitative topic, our marking engine assesses these answers with IB Business Management M (method) and A (accuracy) conventions rather than the essay-style AO bands. An M mark rewards the correct method — the right formula with the right figures substituted in — even if a slip earlier in the working feeds a wrong number into it (follow-through). An A mark rewards the accurate final value WITH its unit. Interpretation marks reward a valid, context-linked comment on what the numbers show. Watch how the marks below attach to method, to accurate values with units, and to a supported interpretation.

sections.question: A business has long-term liabilities of $300,000 and capital employed of $750,000. Its debtors are $40,000 and annual sales revenue is $500,000. Calculate the gearing ratio and the debtor days, and comment on what they show. [5]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**

**Gearing ratio.**
Gearing = (long-term liabilities ÷ capital employed) × 100
= ($300,000 ÷ $750,000) × 100 = **40%**.

**Debtor days.**
Debtor days = (debtors ÷ sales revenue) × 365
= ($40,000 ÷ $500,000) × 365 = **29.2 ≈ 29 days**.

**Comment.** A gearing of 40% shows the business is moderately financed by debt — below the ~50% level usually treated as high — so its financial risk from fixed interest and repayments is manageable while it still uses some debt to fund operations. Debtor days of about 29 show it collects cash from credit customers in under a month, which supports healthy liquidity. Together the two suggest a reasonably financed business with sound short-term cash collection; the picture would be firmer still if compared with previous years and with competitors, and set against how stable its profits are.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 5 marks:**

- **M1 — gearing method:** long-term liabilities over capital employed, correctly substituted as $300,000 ÷ $750,000 × 100.
- **A1 — gearing accuracy:** correct value **40%**, with the % unit.
- **M1 — debtor-days method:** debtors over **sales revenue** × 365, correctly substituted as $40,000 ÷ $500,000 × 365.
- **A1 — debtor-days accuracy:** correct value **≈ 29 days**, with the days unit.
- **A1 — interpretation:** a valid, context-linked comment — e.g. moderate gearing so manageable risk, and fast collection (under a month) supporting liquidity.
- **Follow-through:** if a candidate mis-reads capital employed but then applies the correct gearing method, the method mark still stands; likewise a correct debtor-days method built on a wrong debtors figure keeps its M mark. The A marks, however, require the correct value with its unit — a number with no %, 'times' or 'days' does not earn the accuracy mark, and using COGS instead of sales revenue for debtor days forfeits that method mark.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR calculation this same way — an M mark for the correct method (right formula, right figures), an A mark for the accurate value WITH its unit, and further marks for a valid interpretation, with follow-through on a correct method built on an earlier slip. So always show your working, attach the unit (%, times, days), use the right denominator, and finish with a sentence saying what the number means for the business. The method, the unit and the interpretation are where the marks are won.

These ratios connect straight into the rest of the finance unit. Gearing builds on sources of finance (3.1) and feeds into any evaluation of expansion, borrowing and financial risk. The efficiency ratios extend working-capital and cash-flow management, showing why a profitable firm can still run short of cash. And the limitations of ratio analysis run through every quantitative judgement you make in Business Management. Master the habit built here — pick the right formula, substitute carefully, attach the unit, then interpret in context — and you have the template that earns marks on every calculation-and-comment question in the course.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 quantitative question marked: calculate the gearing and efficiency ratios, attach the units, and interpret them in context

items.label: Corporate Finance Institute: Working Capital Cycle

quickCheck.prompt: A firm's gearing ratio rises from 35% to 65%. What does this most directly indicate?
options: The business now relies more heavily on long-term debt, increasing its financial risk
The business has become more profitable
The business is collecting cash from customers faster
The business has reduced its stock levels
quickCheck.answer: The business now relies more heavily on long-term debt, increasing its financial risk

quickCheck.prompt: Which is the correct formula for debtor (receivables) days?
options: (Debtors ÷ cost of goods sold) × 365
(Debtors ÷ sales revenue) × 365
(Sales revenue ÷ debtors) × 365
(Creditors ÷ sales revenue) × 365

quickCheck.prompt: A business has cost of goods sold of $800,000 and average stock of $100,000. What is its stock turnover in times per year?
options: 8 times
12.5 times
0.125 times
36.5 times

quickCheck.prompt: Which comparison makes a debtor-days figure genuinely meaningful?
options: Comparing it to the firm's previous years, its competitors and the industry norm
Judging it against a fixed rule that any figure over 30 days is always bad
Comparing it to the firm's gearing ratio
Nothing — the raw number alone tells you if it is good
quickCheck.answer: Comparing it to the firm's previous years, its competitors and the industry norm

quickCheck.prompt: For strong liquidity, what is the ideal relationship between a firm's debtor days and creditor days?
options: Debtor days should be lower than creditor days, so cash comes in before it must go out
Debtor days should be higher than creditor days
Debtor days and creditor days should always be exactly equal
Both should be as high as possible
quickCheck.answer: Debtor days should be lower than creditor days, so cash comes in before it must go out
